subject: Math

subject: Math

subject: Math

Your child can add and subtract fractions with different denominators by changing them to have the same denominator first.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Fractions and Decimals — Operations and Problem Solving
coherence_reason: The cluster includes both operations with fractions (including conceptual understanding and word problems) and operations with decimals, which are related but distinct subtopics.

coherence_reason: Most standards focus on comparing decimals to the thousandths, but several refer to comparing whole numbers and some to comparing measurable attributes (length/weight), so there are at least two distinct sub-concepts.

Your child can write linear equations in different forms using one point and the slope or two points.
Your child can write the equation of a line that goes through a point and runs parallel to another line.
Your child can write the equation of a line that is straight up and down or flat and tell if its slope is zero or undefined.
Your child can explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and can write equations for lines through the origin or with a vertical intercept.
Your child can use similar triangles to explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and can write equations for lines through the origin or with a vertical intercept.
Your child can use similar triangles to explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a straight, non-vertical line.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Equations — Slope and Equation Forms
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding slope, using it to write equations of lines, and explaining the properties of linear equations in various contexts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line on a graph and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.
Your child understands that y = mx + b is a rule for a straight-line function and can give examples of functions that are not straight lines.
Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b shows a straight-line function and can tell which functions are straight lines and which are not.
Your child can write the equation for a line through the origin as y = mx and for a line crossing the vertical axis at b as y = mx + b.
Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.
Your child knows the vertical number line on a graph is called the y-axis.
Recognize a linear graph.
Know what a line is.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Functions — Recognizing and Representing Lines
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on recognizing and representing linear functions using y = mx + b, but a few (such as knowing the y-axis and what a line is) are more foundational and slightly outside the main focus.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross, because that point fits both equations.
Your child can draw the shaded area that shows all solutions to two linear inequalities on a graph.
Your child can use a graph to guess the solution to two linear equations in real-life problems.
Your child can solve problems by finding where two linear equations with two variables are true at the same time.
Your child can draw two lines on a graph and find where they cross or decide if they don’t cross.
Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross because that point fits both equations.
Your child can solve systems of two linear equations by graphing or by simple inspection.
Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Systems of Linear Equations — Graphical Solutions
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on solving and interpreting solutions to systems of linear equations graphically, but one standard introduces linear inequalities and another covers basics of the coordinate plane, which are tangential.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve linear equations with one variable.
Your child can add and subtract simple fractions like halves, thirds, and fourths (using pictures), as long as the answer is one or less.
Your child can solve basic algebra equations with one unknown number using addition and subtraction.
Your child can solve linear equations with fractions and decimals, including those needing to expand expressions and combine like terms.
Your child can solve real-life and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.
Your child can pick the right numbers to solve addition and subtraction problems.
Your child can analyze and solve both single linear equations and pairs of linear equations.
Your child can solve simple algebra problems with one unknown number using addition and subtraction.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Equations — Solving One-Variable and Two-Variable Equations
coherence_reason: The standards group both single-variable linear equations (with operations and fractions/decimals) and two-variable systems, plus some standards about basic fraction addition and picking numbers that seem unrelated.
split_topics: Solving single-variable linear equations (including with fractions, decimals, combining like terms)
Solving systems of two linear equations (two variables)
Adding and subtracting simple fractions using pictures
Selecting numbers to solve addition and subtraction problems

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can give examples of one-variable linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution, and show which by simplifying the equation.
Your child can show examples of linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution by simplifying the equations step-by-step.
Your child can tell if a linear equation has one solution, many, or none, and show this by simplifying the equation step by step.
Your child can write and recognize linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solutions by simplifying them step-by-step.
Your child can choose the correct answer from up to three options, even if the correct one is in different places.
Your child can describe and solve linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution by simplifying the equation step-by-step.
Your child can give examples of straight-line equations with one solution, many solutions, or no solutions, and show which by simplifying the equation step-by-step.
Your child can explain why two linear equations can have one solution, no solutions, or many solutions.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Equations — Solutions of One-Variable Equations
coherence_reason: All standards focus on identifying, explaining, and demonstrating the number of solutions (one, none, or many) for one-variable linear equations by simplifying them.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands that kidneys remove waste from the blood and turn it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.

Your child can identify common acids like lemon juice and bases like baking soda and knows their hazards.
Your child learns to tell which common acids are safe and which can be harmful.
Your child knows which common acids are safe and which can be harmful.
Your child can check if security tools like passwords and virus protection are being used correctly to keep information safe.
Your child knows common acids like vinegar and bases like ammonia, and understands which ones can be dangerous.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Science: Acids and Bases — Common Examples and Safety
coherence_reason: The majority of standards focus on identifying common acids and bases and their safety, but one standard is about kidneys and another about cybersecurity, which are separate concepts.
split_topics: Acids and Bases — Common Examples and Safety
Human Body Systems — Kidneys and Waste Removal
Digital Security — Safe Use of Security Tools

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child designs changes to computers to make them easier and better for people to use.

Digital tools

Programming Applications
Your child can describe how computers use smart behaviors like moving robots, understanding speech, and recognizing images.
Your child can explain how people and machines work together to do tasks that neither can do alone.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Science: Technology and Computers — Human-Computer Interaction and Applications
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on how computers and technology interact with humans, their uses, and skills required, though there is some variation from skills to applications to collaboration.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can join group discussions well and understand the information shared.
Knowledge and skills

subject: Science

canonical_name: Grade 8 Science: Multiple Concepts — Gravity, Cells, Energy, Engineering Design, Technology, and Geography
coherence_reason: The standards cover several unrelated science and STEM topics, including gravity, cells, energy, engineering design, technology use, and geography, so they should be split into separate clusters.

subject: Science

subject: Language Arts

subject: Language Arts
